mnemonics.title: DANIS-V Format
mnemonics.content: Remember data type order: Date (java.sql.Date), Array (java.sql.Array), Numeric (BigDecimal), Integer (int), String/Varchar (String), Special (Blob, Clob) - helps remember common getObject() return types

mnemonics.title: G-SET Method Pattern
mnemonics.content: GET methods follow pattern: get + Type (getInt, getString, getDate). SET methods: set + Type + index (setInt, setString, setDate). Always specify parameter index in PreparedStatement

mnemonics.title: NULL Primitive Trap
mnemonics.content: Primitive Getters Return ZERO for NULL: getInt→0, getDouble→0.0, getBoolean→false. Always check wasNull() or use getObject() for nullable primitives

acronyms.usage: API that bridges Java applications with relational databases
Stores binary data like images, documents - use getBytes()/setBytes() or Blob interface
Stores large text data - use getString()/setString() or Clob interface for very large text
SQL commands (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) that manipulate data

visualTips.visualization: Draw a 2-column table: SQL Types (VARCHAR, INTEGER, DATE, TIMESTAMP, BLOB, CLOB, NUMERIC) → Java Types (String, int, java.sql.Date, Timestamp, byte[], Reader, BigDecimal). Color-code by category (numeric=blue, date=yellow, binary=green, text=orange)

Start: Is it NULLable? → Yes → Use getObject(). No → Is it decimal? → Yes → getBigDecimal(). No → Is it date/time? → Use getDate/getTime/getTimestamp. Is it text? → getString. Is it binary? → getBytes. Is it boolean? → getBoolean
Java Object → setXXX(index, value) → Driver converts → SQL type → Database. Draw arrow showing: String 'John' → setString(1,'John') → VARCHAR → Stored in DB

analogies.analogy: Think of JDBC types as translators between two people speaking different languages. Java speaks 'int, String, Date' while SQL speaks 'INTEGER, VARCHAR, DATE'. JDBC drivers are the translators converting each language appropriately.

getObject() is like a universal translator that can handle any language but may lose nuance. Specific getters (getInt, getString) are specialized translators that preserve exact meaning but only work for their specific type.

Primitives in Java cannot be null (int is always 0, boolean always false). So when SQL NULL arrives, JDBC has no choice but to give you 0 or false - it's like receiving 'nothing' but being forced to unwrap it as a default gift.
